model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how to place each shift's operating point along a piecewise-linear production curve, which shifts to open, how many employees to staff on each shift, how many people to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry, and whether to take a lease-rent option for storage. Production draws on material and shift costs, carrying inventory and leasing add storage costs, and hiring and firing add workforce-change costs, all brought to present value by a per-season discount factor. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

sets.name: q
sets.doc: the production seasons of the planning horizon, in chronological order; the first member is the opening season and each later member directly follows the one before it

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the work shifts that can be run within a season

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the ordered breakpoints of a piecewise-linear production curve, numbered from the lowest level upward; consecutive breakpoints bracket the operating segments of the curve

sets.name: prp
sets.doc: the two production-relationship rows: the labor row gives employees required at each breakpoint and the motor row gives motors produced at each breakpoint

sets.name: scp
sets.doc: the two shift-cost rows: the fixed row gives the cost of opening a shift and the labor row gives the per-employee cost of staffing a shift

params.name: d
params.doc: the demand for motors in each season, in motors; zero in seasons with no demand

params.name: lc
params.doc: the leasing cost charged in each season when the lease-rent option is taken, in dollars per season; zero in seasons with no leasing charge

params.name: ei
params.doc: an externally given change in employment that occurs at the start of each season, in employees; zero in seasons with no external change

params.name: mc
params.doc: the material cost incurred per motor produced, in dollars per motor

params.name: sr
params.doc: the space-rental rate charged per motor of inventory held, in dollars per motor

params.name: hc
params.doc: the hiring cost incurred per employee hired, in dollars per employee

params.name: fc
params.doc: the firing cost incurred per employee fired, in dollars per employee

params.name: delt
params.doc: the discount factor applied to each season's costs to bring them to present value; equals 1 in the first season and shrinks geometrically in later seasons

params.name: invmax
params.doc: an upper bound on inventory, in motors, equal to the total demand summed over all seasons

params.name: pr
params.doc: the production-relationship table: pr['motor', l] is the number of motors produced at breakpoint l and pr['labor', l] is the number of employees required at breakpoint l

params.name: sc
params.doc: the shift-cost table: sc['fixed', s] is the fixed cost of opening shift s and sc['labor', s] is the cost per employee of staffing shift s, in dollars

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total discounted cost over the whole horizon, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: dpc
vars.doc: the direct production cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: isc
vars.doc: the inventory storage cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: wfc
vars.doc: the workforce fluctuation cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: src
vars.doc: the space-rental cost in each season, in dollars per season

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the number of motors produced in each season

vars.name: ss
vars.doc: the piecewise-linear production segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; these are the SOS2-type convex weights that place a shift's operating point along the production curve

vars.name: ssb
vars.doc: the 0/1 selector variables that accompany the segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; used to enforce the SOS2 adjacency of the active segment weights

vars.name: inv
vars.doc: the inventory of motors carried at the end of each season

vars.name: lease
vars.doc: the single lease-rent option indicator; equals 1 if the rental option is taken and 0 otherwise

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the total employment in each season, in employees

vars.name: se
vars.doc: the employment on each shift of each season, in employees per shift

vars.name: shift
vars.doc: the shift-use indicator; equals 1 if the shift is opened in that season and 0 otherwise

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the number of employees hired at the start of each season

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of employees fired at the start of each season

description: The direct production cost of each season is the cost of the motors made plus the cost of running the shifts. For each season, take the material cost of every motor produced, add the fixed cost of each shift that is opened and the staffing cost for the employees working that shift, and set the season's direct production cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how to place each shift's operating point along a piecewise-linear production curve, which shifts to open, how many employees to staff on each shift, how many people to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry, and whether to take a lease-rent option for storage. Production draws on material and shift costs, carrying inventory and leasing add storage costs, and hiring and firing add workforce-change costs, all brought to present value by a per-season discount factor. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

description: Production in each season is built up from the piecewise-linear segment weights chosen for the shifts. description: The segment weights on a shift are active only when that shift is opened. For each season and shift, the segment weights summed over all breakpoints must equal the shift's open indicator, so the weights add up to one when the shift runs and to zero when it does not.

description: Inventory carries over from one season to the next as production meets demand. For each season, the ending inventory equals the inventory left from the immediately preceding season plus the motors produced that season minus the demand that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so nothing carries in.

model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total discounted cost over the whole horizon, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: dpc
vars.doc: the direct production cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.doc: the space-rental cost in each season, in dollars per season

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the number of motors produced in each season

vars.name: ss
vars.doc: the piecewise-linear production segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; these are the SOS2-type convex weights that place a shift's operating point along the production curve

vars.name: ssb
vars.doc: the 0/1 selector variables that accompany the segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; used to enforce the SOS2 adjacency of the active segment weights

vars.name: inv
vars.doc: the inventory of motors carried at the end of each season

vars.name: lease
vars.doc: the single lease-rent option indicator; equals 1 if the rental option is taken and 0 otherwise

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the total employment in each season, in employees

vars.name: se
vars.doc: the employment on each shift of each season, in employees per shift

vars.name: shift
vars.doc: the shift-use indicator; equals 1 if the shift is opened in that season and 0 otherwise

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the number of employees hired at the start of each season

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of employees fired at the start of each season

description: The space-rental cost in each season pays for inventory held beyond what the lease option covers. For each season, the space-rental cost must be at least the rental rate applied to the inventory held that season after allowing for the inventory the lease option absorbs when it is taken.

model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how to place each shift's operating point along a piecewise-linear production curve, which shifts to open, how many employees to staff on each shift, how many people to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry, and whether to take a lease-rent option for storage. Production draws on material and shift costs, carrying inventory and leasing add storage costs, and hiring and firing add workforce-change costs, all brought to present value by a per-season discount factor. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

description: The workforce fluctuation cost of each season pays for the hiring and firing done that season. For each season, take the hiring cost for every employee hired and the firing cost for every employee fired, and set the season's workforce fluctuation cost equal to that total expressed in thousands of dollars.

description: Employment evolves from one season to the next through hiring, firing and external changes. For each season, the total employment equals the employment carried from the immediately preceding season plus the people hired, minus the people fired, plus the external employment change for that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so none carries in.

The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

description: The segment weights are tied to the chosen breakpoint selectors so that only weights adjacent to the selected breakpoint can be active. For the lowest breakpoint, that breakpoint's weight cannot exceed its own selector. For the second breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at those two breakpoints. For every higher breakpoint, the weights at that breakpoint and the one just below it together cannot exceed the selectors at that breakpoint and the two just below it. This is applied for every breakpoint, season and shift.

model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how to place each shift's operating point along a piecewise-linear production curve, which shifts to open, how many employees to staff on each shift, how many people to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry, and whether to take a lease-rent option for storage. Production draws on material and shift costs, carrying inventory and leasing add storage costs, and hiring and firing add workforce-change costs, all brought to present value by a per-season discount factor. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

vars.doc: the number of employees fired at the start of each season

description: Production in spring is capped at a fraction of the maximum the shifts could deliver. The motors produced in spring must not exceed eighty percent of the number of shifts times the largest motor output across all breakpoints.
expected_pyomo: model.pup = Constraint(expr=model.p['spring'] <= 0.8*len(model.s)*max(model.pr['motor', l] for l in model.l))

model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how to place each shift's operating point along a piecewise-linear production curve, which shifts to open, how many employees to staff on each shift, how many people to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry, and whether to take a lease-rent option for storage. Production draws on material and shift costs, carrying inventory and leasing add storage costs, and hiring and firing add workforce-change costs, all brought to present value by a per-season discount factor. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

sets.name: q
sets.doc: the production seasons of the planning horizon, in chronological order; the first member is the opening season and each later member directly follows the one before it

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the work shifts that can be run within a season

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the ordered breakpoints of a piecewise-linear production curve, numbered from the lowest level upward; consecutive breakpoints bracket the operating segments of the curve

sets.name: prp
sets.doc: the two production-relationship rows: the labor row gives employees required at each breakpoint and the motor row gives motors produced at each breakpoint

sets.name: scp
sets.doc: the two shift-cost rows: the fixed row gives the cost of opening a shift and the labor row gives the per-employee cost of staffing a shift

params.name: d
params.doc: the demand for motors in each season, in motors; zero in seasons with no demand

params.name: lc
params.doc: the leasing cost charged in each season when the lease-rent option is taken, in dollars per season; zero in seasons with no leasing charge

params.name: ei
params.doc: an externally given change in employment that occurs at the start of each season, in employees; zero in seasons with no external change

params.name: mc
params.doc: the material cost incurred per motor produced, in dollars per motor

params.name: sr
params.doc: the space-rental rate charged per motor of inventory held, in dollars per motor

params.name: hc
params.doc: the hiring cost incurred per employee hired, in dollars per employee

params.name: fc
params.doc: the firing cost incurred per employee fired, in dollars per employee

params.name: delt
params.doc: the discount factor applied to each season's costs to bring them to present value; equals 1 in the first season and shrinks geometrically in later seasons

params.name: invmax
params.doc: an upper bound on inventory, in motors, equal to the total demand summed over all seasons

params.name: pr
params.doc: the production-relationship table: pr['motor', l] is the number of motors produced at breakpoint l and pr['labor', l] is the number of employees required at breakpoint l

params.name: sc
params.doc: the shift-cost table: sc['fixed', s] is the fixed cost of opening shift s and sc['labor', s] is the cost per employee of staffing shift s, in dollars

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total discounted cost over the whole horizon, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: dpc
vars.doc: the direct production cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: isc
vars.doc: the inventory storage cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: wfc
vars.doc: the workforce fluctuation cost in each season, in thousands of dollars per season

vars.name: src
vars.doc: the space-rental cost in each season, in dollars per season

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the number of motors produced in each season

vars.name: ss
vars.doc: the piecewise-linear production segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; these are the SOS2-type convex weights that place a shift's operating point along the production curve

vars.name: ssb
vars.doc: the 0/1 selector variables that accompany the segment weights, indexed by breakpoint, season and shift; used to enforce the SOS2 adjacency of the active segment weights

vars.name: inv
vars.doc: the inventory of motors carried at the end of each season

vars.name: lease
vars.doc: the single lease-rent option indicator; equals 1 if the rental option is taken and 0 otherwise

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the total employment in each season, in employees

vars.name: se
vars.doc: the employment on each shift of each season, in employees per shift

vars.name: shift
vars.doc: the shift-use indicator; equals 1 if the shift is opened in that season and 0 otherwise

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the number of employees hired at the start of each season

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of employees fired at the start of each season

description: Generate the complete constraint set for this model.
